braid.sentence: Before the gates were three young girls, dressed in handsome red uniforms trimmed with gold braid; and as Dorothy approached, one of them said to her: "Why have you come to the South Country?"

receive.sentence: "Let me have your name, and I will ask Glinda if she will receive you."

presentable.sentence: When they were all quite presentable they followed the soldier girl into a big room where the Witch Glinda sat upon a throne of rubies.

ringlet.sentence: Her hair was a rich red in color and fell in flowing ringlets over her shoulders.

upturn.sentence: Glinda leaned forward and kissed the sweet, upturned face of the loving little girl.

bless.sentence: "Bless your dear heart," she said, "I am sure I can tell you of a way to get back to Kansas."

turning.sentence: Turning to the Tin Woodman, she asked, "What will become of you when Dorothy leaves this country?"

grand.sentence: "Over the hill of the Hammer-Heads," he answered, "lies a grand old forest, and all the beasts that live there have made me their King.

evermore.sentence: Then, having used up the powers of the Golden Cap, I shall give it to the King of the Monkeys, that he and his band may thereafter be free for evermore."

tenderly.sentence: She threw her arms around the Lion's neck and kissed him, patting his big head tenderly.

sorrowful.sentence: But she hugged the soft, stuffed body of the Scarecrow in her arms instead of kissing his painted face, and found she was crying herself at this sorrowful parting from her loving comrades.

part.sentence: But she hugged the soft, stuffed body of the Scarecrow in her arms instead of kissing his painted face, and found she was crying herself at this sorrowful parting from her loving comrades.

solemnly.sentence: Dorothy now took Toto up solemnly in her arms, and having said one last good-bye she clapped the heels of her shoes together three times, saying: "Take me home to Aunt Em!"

past.sentence: Instantly she was whirling through the air, so swiftly that all she could see or feel was the wind whistling past her ears.

furiously.sentence: Uncle Henry was milking the cows in the barnyard, and Toto had jumped out of her arms and was running toward the barn, barking furiously.

barnyard.sentence: Uncle Henry was milking the cows in the barnyard, and Toto had jumped out of her arms and was running toward the barn, barking furiously.

stock.sentence: Dorothy stood up and found she was in her stocking-feet.

cabbage.sentence: Home Again Aunt Em had just come out of the house to water the cabbages when she looked up and saw Dorothy running toward her.

fold.sentence: she cried, folding the little girl in her arms and covering her face with kisses.
